topic: Decision Making to Improve Operational Performance
subject: business-studies

items.q: Is 100% capacity utilisation the ideal target for a business?
items.correct: Usually not — at full capacity there is no slack for maintenance, breakdowns or sudden orders, and quality and morale can suffer; a high but not full level is often preferred.
items.misconception: A business should always aim to run at 100% capacity.
items.why: Students assume more utilisation is always better, missing the costs of having no slack and overworked resources.

items.q: Does just-in-time stock control always reduce costs?
items.correct: It cuts storage and waste costs, but it relies on dependable suppliers, sacrifices bulk-buying discounts, and leaves the firm exposed if deliveries fail.
items.misconception: JIT is risk-free because holding no stock can only save money.
items.random_wrong: JIT means stock is delivered just in time for the annual stocktake.
items.why: Students see only the saving side of JIT and ignore supply-chain vulnerability and lost purchasing economies.

items.q: Are quality control and quality assurance the same thing?
items.correct: No — quality control inspects output after production to catch defects, while quality assurance builds prevention into every stage so defects are less likely to occur.
items.misconception: Checking finished products is all a business needs to do to guarantee quality.
items.random_wrong: Quality assurance is a type of insurance policy bought from banks.
items.why: Students conflate inspection with assurance, missing the shift from detecting faults to preventing them.

items.correct: Actual output divided by maximum possible output, times 100
items.misconception: Maximum output divided by actual output, times 100
items.random_wrong: Total output divided by the number of employees
items.why: It's Actual output divided by maximum possible output, times 100. “Maximum output divided by actual output, times 100” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Minimise stock holding by ordering materials only as they are needed
items.misconception: Hold large buffer stocks in case of emergencies
items.random_wrong: Inspect every finished product before despatch
items.why: Correct answer: Minimise stock holding by ordering materials only as they are needed. “Hold large buffer stocks in case of emergencies” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A likely benefit of high capacity utilisation is:
items.correct: Fixed costs are spread over more units, cutting unit costs
items.misconception: More spare capacity to take on sudden new orders
items.random_wrong: Less pressure on machinery and staff
items.why: Correct answer: Fixed costs are spread over more units, cutting unit costs. “More spare capacity to take on sudden new orders” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Continuous improvement in small steps, involving employees
items.misconception: A one-off radical restructuring of the business
items.why: Correct answer: Continuous improvement in small steps, involving employees. “A one-off radical restructuring of the business” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A factory can make 2,000 units a week but currently makes 1,700. What is its capacity utilisation in %?
items.why: The real figure is 85 %. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
